high_value_elements: Correctly names A-delta (fast, sharp) and C fibers (slow, burning/aching) as the primary nociceptive afferents
Correctly identifies substance P and glutamate as primary neurotransmitters at the dorsal horn synapse; names NK-1 receptor for substance P and AMPA/NMDA receptors for glutamate
Correctly describes wind-up: repeated C-fiber input activates NMDA receptor due to removal of Mg2+ block, leading to central sensitization
Identifies PAG (periaqueductal gray) as the primary brainstem origin of descending inhibition; serotonin and norepinephrine as the two descending inhibitory neurotransmitters
Names at least three pathway-level drug targets: opioids at dorsal horn and supraspinal levels, NMDA antagonists at dorsal horn, alpha-2 agonists at dorsal horn, NSAIDs at peripheral transduction
major_concerns: Cannot name the fiber types involved in nociception or confuses A-beta (touch) with nociceptive fibers
Cannot describe what happens at the dorsal horn during wind-up or does not know the NMDA receptor plays a central role
Cannot identify any point in the nociceptive pathway where fellowship-taught drugs act

high_value_elements: Correctly identifies allodynia extending beyond the lesion site as evidence of central sensitization, not peripheral-only disease
Adds an NMDA antagonist: ketamine CRI (approximately 60 mg/L at 5-10 mL/kg/hr IV) for perioperative use, or amantadine 3-5 mg/kg once daily for chronic outpatient management
Adds a gabapentinoid targeting the alpha-2-delta subunit of voltage-gated calcium channels to reduce central sensitization
Correctly identifies that NSAIDs alone are insufficient once central sensitization is established because they act peripherally without blocking dorsal horn NMDA receptor activation
For rabbits, correctly identifies that the spinal cord extends to approximately L6-S1 (compared to approximately S3 in dogs), requiring adjustment for epidurals
major_concerns: Recommends only dose escalation of the NSAID without adding a centrally acting agent
Cannot name specific NMDA antagonist options or their doses
Does not recognize the spatial distribution of allodynia as evidence of central versus peripheral sensitization

high_value_elements: Prioritizes addressing central sensitization early; recognizes that delay allows further neuroplastic changes that are harder to reverse
Correctly applies COAST staging (0-4) framework to guide OA management decisions and escalation thresholds
Identifies that cats' glucuronidation deficiency (Phase II hepatic metabolism) requires exclusion or dose reduction of drugs relying on this pathway
Correctly sequences central sensitization treatment: NSAID for peripheral inflammation PLUS NMDA antagonist PLUS gabapentinoid for central component
major_concerns: Treats established central sensitization the same as acute nociceptive pain without mechanism-based escalation
Does not recognize any species-specific neuroanatomic or pharmacokinetic constraint

high_value_elements: Explains the Mg2+ block mechanism: at resting potential NMDA receptor is blocked by Mg2+; sustained depolarization from repeated C-fiber input removes this block, allowing Ca2+ influx and wind-up
Explains why amantadine is preferred for chronic oral outpatient NMDA blockade versus ketamine which requires IV administration
Explains the evolutionary basis for pain masking in prey species and why this requires proactive multimodal assessment
Explains why cats' glucuronidation deficiency results in prolonged drug half-lives and extended dosing intervals
major_concerns: Cannot explain the Mg2+ block or why NMDA receptor activation requires sustained depolarization
Cannot justify the choice of amantadine versus ketamine for different clinical contexts

high_value_elements: Acknowledges that the exact dose and duration of NMDA antagonist therapy for established central sensitization is not definitively established
Recognizes that validated neurologic assessment tools for pain in exotic species are limited or absent
Identifies when a neuropathic pain presentation warrants specialist consultation
major_concerns: Claims to fully reverse established central sensitization with any single drug
Cannot identify any limit to the applicability of canine neurophysiology data to exotic species

high_value_elements: Distinguishes peripheral sensitization (lowered threshold of peripheral nociceptors from local inflammatory mediators) from central sensitization (NMDA-mediated amplification at dorsal horn) with clinical implications

Discusses neuroplasticity: chronic pain causes structural changes in the dorsal horn that persist beyond the initiating stimulus, explaining why early aggressive analgesia prevents chronic pain development
Integrates Epstein fellowship content on neuropathic pain: allodynia, hyperalgesia, spontaneous pain, and the importance of combining pharmacologic classes targeting different pathway nodes
major_concerns: Cannot distinguish peripheral from central sensitization clinically or pharmacologically
Treats neuropathic pain as identical to acute nociceptive pain in drug selection
